coredata.dc:title: A robust and simple non-enzymatic electrochemical sensor based on carbon dots-metal oxide composite for the detection of metronidazole traces in food products
coredata.dc:creator: Aslam, Hafiza Khadija
Bilal, Sehrish
Mir, Sana
Tabassum, Sobia
Gilani, Mazhar Amjad
Yaqub, Mustansara
Asim, Muhammad
coredata.dc:description: A facile and simple electrochemical composite sensor, CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA, prepared from carbon dots stabilized silver nanoparticles and copper oxide, was used as an electrocatalyst and signal amplifier for the non-enzymatic detection of antibiotic traces in food products. The prepared composite demonstrated excellent stability, sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness. The sensor was constructed by modifying a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA, and the electroanalytical response was determined for the precise determination of metronidazole (MTZ) drug traces in milk. The analytical response signified fast electron transfer and accessibility of several electroactive sites, producing an amplified response for the reduction of MTZ. The quantitative analysis by the sensor revealed a good linear range (10–110 μM), a low limit of detection (7.1 × 10–7 molL−1), and a high sensitivity (1.5 μA μM−1 cm−2). Furthermore, the sensor displayed excellent potential for practical applications, verified by the good recovery of the drug from spiked milk samples.

head.ce:title: A robust and simple non-enzymatic electrochemical sensor based on carbon dots-metal oxide composite for the detection of metronidazole traces in food products

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and simple electrochemical composite sensor, CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA, prepared from carbon dots stabilized silver nanoparticles and copper oxide, was used as an electrocatalyst and signal amplifier for the non-enzymatic detection of antibiotic traces in food products. The prepared composite demonstrated excellent stability, sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness. The sensor was constructed by modifying a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA, and the electroanalytical response was determined for the precise determination of metronidazole (MTZ) drug traces in milk. The analytical response signified fast electron transfer and accessibility of several electroactive sites, producing an amplified response for the reduction of MTZ. The quantitative analysis by the sensor revealed a good linear range (10–110 μM), a low limit of detection (7.1 × 10–7 molL−1), and a high sensitivity (1.5 μA μM−1 cm−2). Furthermore, the sensor displayed excellent potential for practical applications, verified by the good recovery of the drug from spiked milk samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The precise detection of antibiotics residues in animal-derived food is essential for ensuring food safety, regulatory compliance, and the prevention of antibiotic resistance, thereby safeguarding public health and promoting sustainable agriculture practices (Marshall, 2011; Phillips et al., 2004). Metronidazole (MTZ), a nitroimidazole derivative, is a widely antiparasitic and antibiotic drug in both human and veterinary medicine. Its applications range from treating amebiasis, giardiasis, and trichomoniasis to preventing bacterial infections in animals and as a feed additive in aquaculture. However, the prolonged or excessive use of the drug is known to pose genotoxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic effects, prompting many countries to ban its usage as a food additive or in feed processing (Davies & Mafadzean, 1964; Dione et al., 2015; Dunn et al., 2007; Jarrad et al., 2015). Moreover, its indiscriminate use in dairy animals cause contamination of raw milk with its residues, posing potential health risks to the consumers and contributing to antibiotic resistance. Nonetheless, the high solubility of drug in water and lack of biodegradability present technical challenges for accurate detection of MTZ from food samples (Lamp et al., 1999; Li et al., 2014). Thus, there is a pressing need for reliable screening methods to detect antibiotic residues efficiently.

Currently various methods are being practiced for the antimicrobial determination including microbiological assays, gas chromatography, High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Tavakoli et al., 2007), or spectrometric methods (Ho et al., 2005), On the other hand, a lot of research is focused on the development of advanced analytical systems such as Surface Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS) (Benítez-Martínez et al., 2015), Fluorescence (Hatamie et al., 2018), Chemiluminescence (Orooji et al., 2020), and electrochemical approaches (Mao et al., 2017; Wang, Yao, et al., 2019). Nonetheless, many of these methods lack simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and accessibility for real time drug monitoring. In contrast electrochemical sensors incorporating advanced nanomaterials shows great promise due to their simplicity and applicability when compared to other detection methods (Pour et al., 2023).

The robustness and efficiency of an electrochemical sensor is mainly contingent upon the surface architecture of the material used to modify the electrode surface (Ahmad et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2023). A potential nanomaterial integrated as a transducer on the electrode surface influences the signal performance of the devised sensor (Bilal et al., 2021; Bilal et al., 2022; Yaqub et al., 2022). Numerous reports suggest that the chemical composition, structure, size, surface defects and properties of nanomaterials are the critical factors, which can be tuned to obtain multifunctional nanomaterials, which specifically interact with target analyte and provide an amplified signal response (Bilal et al., 2024). Among these, the potential of noble metal nanoparticles has been extensively studied in electrochemical sensing platform owing to their exceptional electrocatalytic properties (Ansari et al., 2021). Notable the nanocomposites of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and carbon dots (CDs) have been recently exploited for diverse applications attributing to their unique chemical and physical properties (Guo, Jin, et al., 2017; Guo, Tang, et al., 2017). Wang reported a Ag-CDs nanocomposite with peroxidase mimicking activity to be used as artificial nanozyme for the detection of uric acid (Wang, Guan, et al., 2019). Gul et al. developed carbon silver nano-assembly to quantify hydrogen peroxide and glucose by UV–visible spectroscopy. (Gul et al., 2020a) In another study Su et al. proposed a green method for the synthesis of AgNPs/N-CDs nanocomposite and explored it SERS characteristics (Su et al., 2016). The incorporation of carbon dots resolves the problems related to stability and recovery of silver nanoparticles and acts as a as a promising stabilizer for AgNPs by virtue of their hydrophilicity and active sites. Thus, the surface functional groups act as structural scaffolds for metal nanoparticles stabilization. Herein, we have incorporated Cu2O nanoparticles to improve the electrochemical conductivity of CDs stabilized AgNPs. Various transition metal oxide nanoparticles have been used in previous studies as a catalytic support and provide additional advantages owing to their ease of preparation, structural reproducibility, and good electrocatalytic properties (Fazio et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2024; Zokhtareh et al., 2023). Copper oxide (Cu2O) based nanocomposite has been reported to possess excellent redox properties, biocompatibility, high stability and conductivity (Luo et al., 2014). Thus Ag-CDs immobilized Cu2O nanocomposite plays a synergistic catalytic role for the oxidation of selected analyte, providing an amplified signal response, and high sensitivity making them suitable electrode materials for electrochemical sensors.

In this study, a novel and sensitive electrochemical system employing CDs stabilized Ag nanoparticles and gallic acid stabilized Cu2O nanoparticles. It was inspected that the CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE provided a multifunctional catalytic platform for the rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of Metronidazole drug as explained in Scheme 1 . The composition, structure, morphology, and electrocatalytic properties of the composite materials were studied thoroughly. Besides, the applicability of the devised method for the real time monitoring of drug were investigated from milk samples. The proposed method presents potential for wider applications including non-invasive drug monitoring from complex biological sample, food safety and security, pharmaceutical industry and catalysis.

Analytical Gallic acid, Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Copper (II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2.2H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) and potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6), grade were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, Corp USA. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from Scharlau chemicals. Pomegranate peels (Punica granatum), were collected from a local market in Lahore (Pakistan). Metronidazole (MTZ) antimicrobial drug was acquired from Brand Direct Health Pharmacy, USAand used without prior purification. 0.1 M phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution, prepared with deionized water from a Millipore Milli-Q purification system, served as the supporting electrolyte for all analytical measurements. A 1 mM metronidazole (MTZ) stock solution was prepared in PBS and stored at 4 °C.

Carbon dots (CDs), CDs-Ag, copper oxide particles were analyzed by UV-VIS spectroscopy via a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Lambda-35, PerkinElmer, America) in the spectral range of 200–700 nm. Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrophotometer was employed to record the PL spectra of CDs and CDs-Ag in the wavelength range of 300 nm to 600 nm using glass cuvettes. Excitation and emission slits were set to a width of 5 nm, the excitation wavelength was set to 370 nm, and all data was interpreted using Cary Eclipse software. The chemical structure of prepared materials was analyzed by using FTIR spectrometer, Nicolet iS50. XRD patterns of the prepared particles were recorded on Rigaku-D/max-2500-PC diffractometer. Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) instrument (Malvern, UK) was used to determine the size and charge of CDs, CDs-Ag, Cu2O-GA, and CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA samples. Surface topography and morphology of the synthesized samples were performed by Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) Apreo S from ThermoFischer Scientific Eindhoven, The Netherlands. All the images were observed at 5 kv at different magnifications. EDS analysis was performed using TESCAN Vega3 LMU-variable pressure Electron Microscope. Further, the synthesized materials were examined for the electrochemical measurements using electrochemical potantiostat/workstation from GAMRY instruments, USA. It was connected to Ag/AgCl/KCl and Platinum wire electrodmagnifications.ce and the auxiliary electrodes, respectively. The glassy carbon electron (GCE) was employed as a working electrode for this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots (CDs) by a green method
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using pomegranate peels as a renewable source[33]. The washed peels were dried in the shade at room temperature and then carbonized in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the carbonized peels were ground into a fine powder and sieved. 500 mg of the sieved powder was suspended in 100 ml of deionized water and sonicated for 50 min to ensure good dispersion. Finally, the suspension was heated in a microwave oven at 700 W for 15 min using 30-s on/off intervals. The reaction solution was cooled to room temperature and filtered using filter paper, followed by re-filtration with a PTEF 0.20 μm syringe filter. The resulting supernatant, containing the CDs solution, was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. (Gul et al., 2020b).

The AgNO3 solution (0.01 M) in deionized water was added dropwise into the as prepared CDs solution. The solution mixture was vigorously stirred at 25 °C for two hours. The dark brownish color of the solution was converted to yellowish brown, which is an indication that CDs stabilized AgNPs (CDs-Ag) were formed. The obtained solution was filtered with a PTEF syringe filter (0.20 μm) and stored in refrigerator at 4 °C. (Gul et al., 2020b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Gallic acid stabilized Copper Oxide particles (Cu2O-GA)
ce:section.ce:para: Cu2O particles were prepared in alkaline medium using gallic acid as a stabilizing agent and sodium hydroxide as reducing agents. Gallic acid solution of 2.5 mmol (0.425 g 0.5 M) was prepared in 5 ml of NaOH (1.6 M) solution. Alongside 2.5 mmol (0.426 g-0.5 M) CuCl2.2H2O solution was prepared in 5 ml of DI water. The prepared copper solution was added drop wise into the gallic acid solution. The mixture was gently stirred for 2 h at constant temperature of at 90 °C A thick black colored material was obtained. The mixture was then centrifuged at 10,000 xg for 10 min. The nanoparticles were collected from the bottom of the centrifuge tube after separating the supernatant. The particles were washed five times with DI water and dark brown nanoparticles were dried in an oven at 37 °C for 24 h. The as-synthesized solid was referred to as Gallic acid stabilized Cu2O nanoparticles.

The CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite was prepared by mixing of 4 mg of as-prepared Cu2O-GA particles with 0.1 ml of as prepared CDs-Ag solution. The solution mixture was sonicated for 1 h for homogenized dispersion of nanoparticle. The obtained product was referred to CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite.

For the fabrication of non-enzymatic sensor for MTZ monitoring, glassy carbon electrode was modified with CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite. Prior to each modification, GCE was polished with alumina powder of 0.3 and 0.05 μm slurry on microcloth pads until its surface looked like a mirror. The excess alumina particles were removed by gentle washing with DI water. The working electrode was then sonicated for 5 min in DI water. To modify the GCE electrode, 4.2 μL of (5%) Nafion was added in 100 μL of as-prepared CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite suspension to avoid leaching of composite from the electrode's surface during electrochemical measurements. The mixture was sonicated for 15 min and then 5 μL of this suspension was drop casted on GCE active surface with the help of a micropipette and allowed to dry at room temperature for about two hours to get the suspension uniformly distributed on GCE surface. The surface modified electrode was used for further electrochemical sensing studies.

The reliability of the devised method for the detection of MTZ residues in real samples was scrutinized by performing the analysis on fresh milk and tetra pack milk samples. Before analysis, all milk samples were ultracentrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min to remove any foulants and then filtered to obtain skimmed milk. A standard addition method was employed to spike milk samples with MTZ. For that purpose, 200 μL of milk sample was mixed with PBS Buffer (pH 7.0). The known amount of MTZ was spiked in the milk samples which was analyzed by cyclic voltammetry. The recovery percentage of each MTZ concentration was calculated from the regression equation of the calibration plot.

An economical and environmentally sustainable method was used to synthesize carbon dots (CDs). CDs inherit many properties from the material used in their synthesis. They were synthesized using a sustainable source, pomegranate peels. Peels, which are rich in bioactive compounds, serve as a renewable and biocompatible material for CD synthesis. The microwave-assisted solvothermal method was used for its rapid and economical synthesis. To confirm the successful synthesis, various techniques were employed. (Gul et al., 2020a; Shen et al., 2013) UV spectroscopy revealed its characteristic light absorption spectra, while fluorescence spectroscopy confirmed its fluorescent nature. FTIR identified the functional groups on the CD surface, XRD showed its characteristic peak. Additionally, dynamic light scattering data measurements and FESEM also supported the successful synthesis.

Precursors used for CDs synthesis with specific functional groups can leave their mark on the surface during synthesis. This can lead to various functionalities in CDs, including antioxidant, antibacterial, antifungal, biocompatible, and targeted binding capabilities, along with catalytic activities. (Batool et al., 2022; Maruthapandi et al., 2021) In this study, pomegranate peels, a renewable source rich in bioactive compounds, were used to synthesize CDs. Pomegranate peels constitute a significant portion (40–50%) of the fruit and contain a wealth of phenolic compounds, including flavonoids (anthocyanins, catechins) and hydrolyzable tannins including punicalin, pedunculagin, gallic acid, and ellagic acid. These compounds are known for their redox properties and contribute significantly to the reducing and antioxidant activity of the peels. (Gul et al., 2020a; Shen et al., 2013) The FTIR spectrum of the synthesized CDs revealed signals for various functional groups characteristic of flavonoid compounds and aromatic structure with abundant surface amino and phenol hydroxyl groups, which likely served as electron donors for the reduction of Ag + to elemental silver. (Shen et al., 2013; Smaoui et al., 2019) The synthesized CDs nanoparticles were then used as reducing and stabilizing agents for the synthesis of Ag nanoparticles. In the reaction mixture, no additional reducing agents were added. FTIR, XRD, UV, dynamic light scattering data, FESEM and EDS analyses supported the successful synthesis of Ag nanoparticles and demonstrated the reducing potential of the prepared CDs. (Gul et al., 2020a; Gul et al., 2020b; Shen et al., 2013) Gallic acid stabilized Cu2O particles were prepared in alkaline medium by heating at 90 °C. Gallic acid was used as stabilizing agents and NaOH was used as reducing agent. The CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite was prepared by mixing and sonicating Cu2O-GA particles with the as-prepared CDs-Ag solution. There is great capability for developing hydrogen bonding, Van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions, π-π interactions, and coordination bonds among CDs-Ag and Cu2O-GA particles. These interactions are the primary forces that could occur in such types of composites. Electrostatic interactions might develop between amino groups present on the CDs-Ag surface and the carboxylic groups of gallic acid present on Cu2O-GA. Hydrogen bonding could form among hydroxyl groups present in gallic acid-stabilized Cu2O-GA, oxygen atoms on the Cu2O, and hydroxyl groups and amino groups present on CDs-Ag particles. Since CDs-Ag particles are graphitic in nature, there is the possibility to develop π-π interactions between the aromatic regions of gallic acid and the graphitic surfaces of the CDs-Ag. Functional groups on the CDs-Ag surfaces could develop coordination bonds with Cu2O particles. These interactions can lead to the development of interactions among them and the formation of a composite structure. Characterization techniques such as FTIR, XRD, FESEM,EDS and zeta potential analysis supported the successful synthesis of the composite.

The optical properties of CDs, CDs-Ag, Cu2O-GA, and CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA samples are examined by UV-VIS spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 1a. The UV/VIS absorption spectra of CDs show an absorbance peak at 280 nm, attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds, and the absorption at 367 nm is due to the n-π* transition occurring due to the presence of CO bonds. (Gul et al., 2020b) The UV/VIS absorption spectrum of the CDs-Ag nanoparticles shows a red shift in the absorption peak in the visible region around 421 nm, attributed to the local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect of spherical AgNPs. (Mansouri & Sjajoc, 2009) The unmodified AgNPs show the SPR peak at ∼390 nm. This occurs due to the delocalization of electrons at the surface of agglomerated CDs-Ag, leading to a red shift elicited by LSPR transfer to lower energy. (Gayen & Palchoudhury, 2019; Liu et al., 2017; Mai et al., 2019; Wang, Guan, et al., 2019). The UV spectra of gallic acid-stabilized copper oxide particles show a peak at 265 nm indicating the presence of gallic acid, and a strong peak at 280 nm and a weak peak around 400 nm representing the formation of Cu2O particles. (Errokh et al., 2021; Tseng et al., 2011) The comparison of the UV-VIS spectra of the CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite with its individual components verifies its formation. It possesses signals of both CDs-Ag and Cu2O-GA. (See Figure 1

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs and CDs-Ag are measured at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm (Fig. 1b). The PL intensity of the CDs is slightly higher, and the maximum emission intensity is observed at 435 nm, while CDs-Ag show a slightly redshifted peak at 440 nm. This difference may be attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. (Abdolmohammad-Zadeh et al., 2021) In the CDs-Ag sample, energy absorbed by the CDs might be transferred non-radiatively to the silver nanoparticles, which leads to a reduction in the peak intensity of the CDs-Ag. The slight redshift in CDs-Ag might be due to the interaction between CDs and Ag nanoparticles, which could modify the electronic structure and energy levels of the CDs. The PL spectra support the successful interaction of CDs with silver nanoparticles. Fig. 1c demonstrates the inherent fluorescent properties of CDs, which are visible under UV light, and these properties are preserved in the CDs-Ag sample.

XRD spectra of CDs, CDs-Ag, Cu2O-GA, and CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA are shown in Fig. 1d. XRD spectra are measured in the range of 15° to 70°. The XRD pattern of as-prepared CDs shows a characteristic broad peak at 24°, representing the successful synthesis of CDs. (Kumar et al., 2014) The XRD pattern of the CDs-Ag sample shows the formation of face-centered cubic Ag nanoparticles. The signals are present at 32°, 38°, 46°, 64.2°, and 76.7°, assigned to the (122), (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes, respectively. (Ghaseminezhad et al., 2012; Wei et al., 2021) CDs in the Ag-CDs samples exhibit peaks with 2θ values of 27.5°, 40.7°, 44.1°, and 54.5°. These signals are related to the (002), (100), (101), and (004) planes corresponding to the graphite-2H structure and reveal the presence of CDs in the Ag solution. [51, 52]The shifting of the CDs peaks to 27.8° and the appearance of new signals indicate the interaction between silver nanoparticles and the CDs surface. XRD patterns of the copper oxide sample are depicted by the peaks at 2θ values of 29.8°, 36.6°, 42.3°, and 61.2°, corresponding to the planes (110), (111), (200), and (220), respectively. These planes match well with JCPDS Card No. 01–078-2076 for particles of cuprous oxide (Cu2O). (Berra et al., 2018; Botsa et al., 2019)

The prepared samples CDs, CDs-Ag, Cu2O-GA, CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA were characterized with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to identify their chemical structure. (Fig. 1e and f) The CDs spectra shows broad stretching vibration band around 3250 cm−1 with a shoulder at 3380 cm−1 indicates the presence of OH and NH groups, and band broadening suggests intermolecular and intramolecular hydrogen bonding. The peaks at 2930 cm−1 and 2880 cm−1 correspond to aliphatic CH stretching, while the peak at 1431 cm−1 indicates both aliphatic and aromatic CH bending vibrations. The peaks at 1600 cm−1 and 1725 cm−1 suggest the presence of CN, CO, and N-C=O bonds from carbonyl derivatives. The signals at 1430 and 1600 cm−1 represented aromatic moieties in the carbon core. Additionally, absorbance bands between 1430 and 1329 cm−1 likely correspond to the bending frequencies of OH from alcohols and carboxylate groups, while those between 1210 and 1050 cm−1 suggest CO stretching vibrations of esters, alcohols, and aliphatic ethers. The peaks at 1100 cm−1 can be attributed to both CN stretching of aliphatic amines and C-O-C stretching and C-OH bending of glycosidic bonds. 1200 cm−1 and 1245 cm−1 represented aromatic amines. Signals at 700–915 cm−1 is primarily associated with out-of-plane CH bending vibrations in aromatic and alkenes.

FTIR signals of CDs supported the presence of flavonoid and aromatic structure with abundant surface amino and phenol hydroxyl groups, which likely served as electron donors for the reduction of Ag+ to elemental silver. (Shen et al., 2013; Smaoui et al., 2019) The hydroxyl phenolic groups are reductive and can induce the growth of metal nanoparticles, resulting in a quinone-like structure. (Shen et al., 2013) In the FTIR spectra of CDs-Ag, the increased intensity of signals at 1600 cm−1 supported the presence of CO in the quinone structure of the CDs-Ag sample. The decrease in intensity at 1230 cm−1 supported the consumption of aromatic −NH₂ groups during the reduction process. The intensity of some other vibrational bands of CDs-Ag also decreased compared to the vibrational bands of CDs. The intensity of signals at 1725 cm−1 and 850–915 cm−1 decreased. Signals at 1170 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 shifted to 1180 cm−1 and 1225 cm−1, respectively. These changes supported that amine, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and aromatic bonds of CDs were involved in the stabilization of Ag particles. (Gul et al., 2020a)

The FTIR spectrum of gallic acid shows signals around 3200–3600 cm−1 representing the OH groups and at 1700 cm−1 corresponding to the CO stretching vibration of the carboxylic acid. Signals in the 1600–1450 cm−1 region are attributed to CC stretching vibrations in the aromatic ring. Signals in the 1300–1000 cm−1 region contain several peaks associated with CO stretching and OH bending vibrations from both phenolic (OH) and carboxylic acid (COOH) groups, and signals below 1000 cm−1 are associated with various bending and deformation vibrations of CH in the aromatic ring. The FTIR spectrum of Cu2O-GA shows signals at 650 cm−1 and 740 cm−1 representing the vibrational signals of the CuO bond. (Botsa et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018) Signals between 700 and 915 cm−1 are associated with out-of-plane CH bending vibrations of the aromatic ring. The aromatic ring CC stretching vibrations appear in the 1600–1450 cm−1 region in the Cu2O-GA sample. The disappearance of signals at 1710 cm−1, 1017 cm−1, 868 cm−1, and 694 cm−1 in Cu2O -GA sample compared to gallic acid supports the participation of gallic acid in the stabilization of Cu2O particles.

The FTIR spectrum of the CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite exhibits signals characteristic of both CDs-Ag and Cu2O-GA. A broad stretching band around 3200 cm−1 shows bonding vibrations of OH and NH groups. Signals at 3000 cm−1 represent NH stretching vibrations, while 2910 cm−1 and 2840 cm−1 represent CH stretching. Signals around 1570 and 1400–1000 cm−1 are attributed to vibrational modes associated with CO, CO, CN, CC, CC, OH, and NH bonds of the Cu2O-GA acid and CDs-Ag components. Specific signals include NH bending at 1571 cm−1, CH bending at 1431 cm−1, 1450 cm−1, and 1490 cm−1, OH bending of alcohol and carboxylate groups at 1450–1326 cm−1, CN and CO stretching vibrations of amine, ester, and alcohol at 1275 cm−1, 1180 cm−1, and 1070 cm−1, CN stretching of aliphatic amines at 1090 cm−1, CC bending at 1020 cm−1, and CH bending of aromatic groups and CuO stretching and bending vibrations at 745 cm−1 and 695 cm−1.

Distinct shifts and intensity changes are observed in the FTIR spectra of the CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite. A decrease in intensity at 825 cm−1 indicates reduced aromatic CH bending, while increased intensity at 695 and 740 cm−1 suggests enhanced CH bending and CuO vibrations. Shifts in CuO (880 to 890 cm−1) and CH (1050, 1110 to 1020, 1070 cm−1) vibrations further support interactions between the organic and inorganic components. Additionally, a shift from 1500 to 1490 cm−1 and a new signal at 1450 cm−1 highlight changes in the aromatic environment within the composite. Overall, the shifts and intensity changes in the FTIR peaks suggest various interactions, including hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and π-π interactions, involved in the composite's formation.

FESEM is utilized to examine the surface morphology of synthesized CDs, CDs-Ag, Cu2O-GA, and the CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite (Fig. 2 ). FESEM reveals clusters of uniformly shaped, round CDs with an irregular aggregation pattern. CDs-Ag exhibit a round shape with uniformly distributed silver nanoparticles on their surface, which is crucial for optical and electrical applications, particularly in electrochemical sensing. Cu2O-GA particles are block-shaped with rough and porous morphology, likely due to the presence of gallic acid. Gallic acid might act as a stabilizing agent during Cu2O synthesis, influencing particle growth and morphology. It could also bind to the Cu2O surface, altering its surface chemistry and leading to a rough texture. Additionally, Cu2O exhibits a strong tendency to agglomerate due to strong interparticle interactions. The SEM image of CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA confirms the successful formation of the composite, showing a well-dispersed distribution of CDs-Ag on the block-shaped Cu2O particles. These observations are supported by FESEM images at different magnifications, as shown in Figs. S1-S4 in the supporting information file. EDS results for CDs-Ag display signals for carbon and silver, while Cu2O-GA shows signals for carbon and copper. (Fig. 2) The EDS analysis confirms the presence of C, O, Cu, and Ag in the CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite, supporting the formation of the composite.

Dynamic light scattering data reveal the size and charge of CDs, CDs-Ag, Cu2O-GA, and their composite CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA (Table 1 and Fig. S5). The CDs derived from pomegranate peels are 110 nm in size with a slightly negative charge of −11.1 mV, which arises from the surface functional groups inherited from the pomegranate peel precursors. CDs-Ag are larger, with a size of 226 nm, indicating that multiple CDs are involved in stabilizing the Ag particles. Cu2O-GA particles are slightly larger, with a size of 263 nm and a negative charge of −23.7 mV, representing the gallic acid on their surface. The composite, formed by combining CDs-Ag and Cu2O-GA, is 1100 nm and has a significantly more negative charge of −56.5 mV. This larger size and charge suggest that multiple particles assemble into a single composite unit, with the combined negative charges contributing to the overall increase in size and charge. These findings highlight the impact of constituent interactions and the successful synthesis of CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA. Sizes are slightly larger in DLS compared to FESEM, which might be due to strong interactions among the particles that lead to aggregation.

The electrochemical properties of composite modified electrode were studied by employing cyclic voltammetry (CV) and impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The kinetic parameters and diffusional aspects of the modified electrodes were further investigated through the implication of EIS. EIS quantitatively determines the diffusion properties of surface-confined systems towards the penetration of some electroactive species ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in this case). Impedance graphs were then interpreted in terms of The Randles equivalent circuit as mentioned below in the Scheme 2 .

Impedance is infact the amount of resistance faced by any redox probe e.g. [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- in this case, and the obtained data could be observed as complex impedence spectra. For this, the redox behavior of probe molecule was evaluated using bare GCE, Cu2Oand CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA in 5 mM K4[Fe(CN)6]3−/4- redox probe in 0.1 M KCl electrolyte at a scan sweep of 50 mV/s as shown in Fig. 3
A. what can be seen is a reduction in peak current values and increase in peak to peak separation value (ΔE) of the redox couple associated to the electrochemical activity of Fe-centres of probe in case of Cu-Oxide/GCE Fig. 3A (b) in comparison to the bare GCE Fig. 3A (a) suggesting an overall decrease in the electric conductivity of the coated film. However, with CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA modified electrode i.e. Fig. 3A (a), a well-defined reversible redox couple with higher peak currents and lesser ΔE value can be observed indicating a higher conductivity of the CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite.
Here, Rs is the solution resistance, Rct represents the charge transfer resistance, Zw denotes the Warburg impedance and Cdl shows the double layer capacitance.

Fig. 3B, demonstrates the overlayed Nyquist plots for bare GCE, Cu-Oxide/GCE, CD-Ag/GCE and CD-Ag@Cu
2
O-GA/GCE. What can be seen is the increase in charge transfer resistance RCT value with the anchoring of CuONPs and CD-Ag onto the surface of electrode as compared to the bare electrode ( Fig. 3B), however, CD-Ag@Cu
2
O-GA/GCE exhibited a lesser resistivity ( Fig. 3B, curve a). The results suggested that CD-Ag@Cu
2
O-GA nanocomposite exhibits lesser resistance or higher conductivity as compared to Cu-oxide and CD-Ag to the current flow indicating its enhanced charge-transfer kinetics in electrochemical analysis. It indicates that the CD-Ag@Cu
2
O-GA/GCE poses a rapid e− tranfer rate which may be due to the fact that the accumulative or synergistic effect of nanocomposite causes the decrement in the resistance offered by the electrode interface and enhances the overall conductivity of the system which is quite complimentary for an electrochemical reaction. The results of EIS are in accordance with CV responses of the same electrodes.

To further explain these interpretations, impedance graphs of bare GCE, Cu-Oxide/GCE and CD-Ag@Cu
2
O-GA/GCE in 0.1 M PBS have also been presented in fig. S6. the obtained results further reinforced the findings of above mentioned impedance analysis in K4[Fe(CN)6]3−/4- redox probe in KCl.

Furthermore, the electroactive surface area of the composite modified electrodes was calculated by recording CVs at various different scan rates in 5 mM [Fe(CN)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic performance and detection of MTZ.
ce:section.ce:para:
A sensor's efficiency can be explained in terms of its ability to selectively respond towards the specific analyte. The electroanalytical behavior of the designed sensor was explored for the detection of MTZ drug using CV in 0.1 M PBS. The electrocatalytic response of the CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE was compared with bare GCE, Cu-Oxide/GCE and CDs-Ag/GCE modified electrodes in the presence of a single concentration of the drug MTZ. It can be seen in Fig. 5 A , the peak currents obtained for the bare GCE has shown very low catalytic current as compared to Cu-Oxide/GCE and CDs-Ag/GCE, however a a higher peak current at − 0.42 V can be observed with CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE, suggesting a superior electrocatalytic performance of this nanoassembly for MTZ detection. A comparison bar plot has also been provided to compare the difference between the catalytic peak currents of integrated electrodes that proves a high catalytic activity of the proposed composite as demonstrated in Fig. 5 B. The enhanced electrocatalytic activity of CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA based sensing assembly is attributed to the presence of several negatively charged active sites on its surface, and π-electrons delocalization causes it to efficiently interact with MTZ. The mechanism of electron transfer is demonstrated in Eq. (2).
(2)

Image 1

The influence of the varying pH on MTZ reduction was investigated at a pH range from 2 to 8 and the influence of pH on the reduction peak current (Ipc) and peak potential (Epc) of MTZ was monitored through CV. With a subsequent increase in pH, the Ipc significantly displaced towards negative peak potentials (Fig. S7 A) showing the participation of H+ ions during the redox reaction occuring at the interface of electrode. The plot between pH and reduction peak potentials (Epc) exhibited a linear behavior with the slope value of −34.57 (R2 = 0.9907) as shown in Fig. S7 (B). The m/n ratio can be calculated by using this slope value by exploiting Nernst Equation which is;
(3)
dE / dpH = − 59.1
m / n
mVpH − 1
here m & n represents the no. of electrons and protons involved in this electrochemical reaction. By using the above mentioned equation no.3, m/n ratio was found to be −57 mV/pH which is bit close to the theoretical Nernst value i.e. -59 mV/pH, hence explaining that the equal number of e− & H+ are involved in this reaction.

Figure S7 (C) demonstrates the plot between the pH of electrolyte and catalytic current (ICAT) of MZN reduction. It can be observed that current value was predominant at pH 7.0 with the maximum peak current of −51 μA for the same concentration of MTZ at 0.05 Vs−1 scan rate. Therefore, pH 7.0 phosphate buffer (0.1 M) was used as optimized electrolyte to carry out rest of the electro-analytical measurements.

A calibration curve for MTZ reduction was developed by recording CVs at different MTZ concentrations in 0.1 M PBS, pH 7.0, 50 mVs−1 scan rate at RT as explained in Fig. S8 (A). The sensor was effectively able to detect MTZ at a linear range of 0.5–1.5 mM (R2 = 0.9839), as shown in Fig. S8 (B). The detection limit (LOD) was determined from the standard deviation of the lowest response and the slope of the curve, which came out to be 0.2 μM. A low LOD and high sensitivity signifies that the fabricated sensor exhibits a useful design for the efficient detection of MTZ drug for real time applications.

Moreover, the effect of scan rate on the electrocatalytic performance of CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE was studied by varying scan rate from 0.1 to 3 Vs−1, with 0.1 mM MTZ in 0.1 M PBS, pH 7.0. As presented in Fig. S8 (C) a continuous increase in cathodic peak current can be observed with increasing scan rate. The absence of anodic peaks has indicated that MTZ undergoes an irreversible reaction and the Ipc shifts towards more negative potential confirming the irreversible nature of MTZ reduction reaction. In addition to this, a linear correlation was observed between Ipc and square root of scan rate with correlation coefficient of 0.9681 suggesting that the reduction of MTZ on the CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE surface is a diffusion-controlled process Fig. S8 (D). it was further confirmed by ploting a graph between log Ipc and log v,as mentioned in fig. S8 (E), which resulted into a straight line curve with slope vale of 0.6 which is very close to 0.5 indicating a diffusion-controlled nature of the MTZ electrochemical reduction at CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPV for the detection of MTZ using CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) is considered more sensitive as compared to CV to determine very low levels of analytes such as organic pollutants, metal ions, drugs etc. Therefore, the analytical response of the prepared sensor for the detection of MTZ was also analyzed through DPV with potential range from to −1 to 1 V, under optimized 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0), and a varying concentration of MTZ from 0 μM to 150 μM. Fig. 6
(A) shows a subsequent increase in cathodic peak currents in the presence of increasing molar concentrations of MTZ. Moreover, a good linear response was obtained between reduction peak current and concentrations of MTZ, with the correlation coefficient to be 0.9953 (Fig. 6 B). The LOD and sensitivity was calculated from the obtained slope value of calibration plot and standard deviation of blank, which came out to be 7.1 × 10−6 m.mol.L−1 and 1.5 μA.μM
−1
.cm
−2 .

Besides, the analytical performance was compared with previously reported MTZ sensors, as displayed in Table 2
. After comparison with a number of modified electrodes, we can say that CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE possess much better electrocatalytic activity, a good linear range and a low LOD which suggests that the proposed sensor is very promising for the real time monitoring of MTZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MTZ from Real Milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability of CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE, the percentage recoveries of MTZ traces from milk samples was investigated. Among all the foods obtained from food animals, milk is perhaps the most intensely regulated item. Because of the lipophilic characteristics, antibiotics can undoubtedly accumulate in higher levels in milk samples. The inescapable utilization of milk makes it important to control levels of antibacterial drug residues in milk. As shown in Table 3 , the percentage recoveries were up 93% (RSD < 5%) from raw milk samples and up to 96.7% from tetra packaged milk samples (RSD < 4%). It can be concluded from the results that the proposed sensor is highly capable and simple for monitoring of MTZ contamination in natural samples.

The electrocatalytic efficiency of this sensor was also validated by secrutinising and comparing its recovery values with the values acquired by employing the HPLC method as mentioned in Table 4 . What can be observed is that the values are comparable ranging from 98.5% to 102%.

Selectivity is a very important parameter to influence the efficiency of a sensor. The capacity of fabricated sensor I.e. CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE to selectively detect MTZ was estimated by determining the current response of fabricated electrode in the absence of MTZ and in the presence of various other potential interfering moities. What can be seen from the results that the presence of 100-fold concentration of AMX (amoxicillin), CIP (ciprofloxacin), CHP (chloramphenicol), DFS (disclofenic sodium), DA (dopamine), AA (ascorbic acid), NO3− and Ca2+ did not produce any notable change in the reduction current of MTZ as explained in Fig. 7 . This suggested that the devised sensor is highly selective towards MTZ and can be successfully applied for the specific detection of MTZ in real samples.

The reproducibility and reliability of the fabricated sensing assembly was evaluated by recording the five electrocatalytic responses of integrated modified electrodes towards the same concentration of analyte i.e. MTZ under the same measuring parameters.

The calculated RSD (%) was <5.0 illustrating a good reproducibility of the fabricated sensor. Furthermore, the repeatability of the sensor was investigated after recurring application (n = 5) of modified electrode and relative standard deviation was found out to be <3%, corresponding to a good reproducibility character of the fabricated sensor.

Long-term stability of CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA/GCE based sensor was evaluated by monitoring the electrocatalytic current attributed to the MTZ reduction over a period of 30 days by employing DPV technique. A percentage loss of 3%, 5.2%, 7.9% and 9.2% was observed for the MTZ reduction peak current over the four weeks consecutively as mentioned in fig. S9, hence indicating a good stability.

CDs-Ag@Cu2O-GA composite was prepared as a synergistic electro-catalyst for the selective detection of antimicrobial drug MTZ in the milk samples by an electrochemical assay. Carbon dots functionalized silver nanoparticles embedded Cu2O composite was synthesized by eco-friendly solvothermal method. Under optimum conditions, the fabricated electrode displayed a good linear relationship for MTZ and a low limit of detection. In addition, CDs-Ag/Cu2O composite was successfully utilized to detect MTZ from real milk samples and it also exhibited good stability, reproducibility and high selectivity to interfering species.
